mnemonics.title: SQL Transaction Commands
mnemonics.content: C-R-S: COMMIT saves, ROLLBACK reverts, SAVEPOINT splits - Think 'Can Really Save' transactions

mnemonics.title: View Types
mnemonics.content: Simple = Single table, no aggregates (S-S-A). Complex = Multiple tables, aggregates, may not update (M-M-N).

mnemonics.title: Trigger Timing
mnemonics.content: B-A-I: BEFORE fires pre-event, AFTER fires post-event, INSTEAD OF replaces the operation

acronyms.usage: Basic database operations performed on tables and can be controlled through views and privileges
Commands like GRANT and REVOKE that control access to database objects
Commands like COMMIT, ROLLBACK, and SAVEPOINT that manage transactions
Standard language for managing relational databases with additional features like views, triggers, procedures

visualTips.visualization: Picture a view as a 'window' or 'filtered glass' showing specific data from tables without storing the data itself. The actual tables are behind the glass.
Think of a trigger as a 'tripwire' or 'alarm system' that gets activated automatically when a specific database event (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) occurs on a table.
Imagine a transaction as a path with checkpoints (savepoints). If something goes wrong, you can return to any checkpoint rather than starting over from the beginning.
Think of a sequence as an automated ticket dispenser that gives sequential numbers one after another, useful for generating unique IDs.

analogies.analogy: Like a戴上不同滤镜的相机 - shows only certain aspects of data without duplicating the original scene
Like a smoke detector - automatically responds to specific events without being manually activated each time
Like an ATM PIN generator - produces sequential numbers on demand for unique identification
Like different levels of access cards in a building - some people can only enter certain areas, others have full access
